macro_summary: The S&P 500 edged lower Wednesday, closing at 749.6 (-0.2%) as optimism from a draft US‑Iran Hormuz ceasefire framework offset by renewed tariff threats and hawkish Fed signals. WTI plunged 2.2% to $90.14 after Iran state TV reported an unofficial MOU to reopen the Strait of Hormuz within one month — the lowest oil price in over a month. Semiconductor names continued their historic melt‑up, with Micron surpassing $1 trillion market cap and SK Hynix surging 9.3%, yet the broad market remained cautious under Fed Chair Warsh's inflation‑fighting posture.
commodities_note: WTI fell sharply to $90.14 (‑2.2%) on the Hormuz ceasefire draft; gold slipped 0.5% to $4,474; US10Y yield held at 4.48%. The DXY was nearly flat at 99.19.

singapore.summary: The Singapore bank cluster remains overbought and extended, with DBS and OCBC RSI in the 70s and all three banks above their 50‑day SMAs. NIM compression is entrenched by US10Y at 4.48% and MAS S$NEER policy; the next relief trigger is a sustained 10Y break below 4.20% or the October MAS review. OCBC continues to outperform on net interest margin resilience, while UOB lags by 2.8% over the month — a divergence that could close if UOB's NIM stabilizes.
singapore.thesis_status: Structurally pressured — dual NIM headwinds persist until rate or S$NEER policy shifts.
singapore.key_development: No change in fundamentals; Singapore Q1 GDP 6% y/y was positive but did not alter the rate outlook.

semiconductors.summary: The semiconductor cluster remains in extreme divergence: memory and AI networking stocks (MU, SK Hynix, NOK, BE) are surging to all‑time highs while core logic names (NVDA, AVGO, TSM) lag or pull back. SOXX hit a record 560.96 (+12.9% 5d) driven by memory super‑cycle optimism, but 15 out of 26 watchlist tickers are in extreme extension (>20% above 50‑day). This is a momentum‑driven melt‑up with no fundamental margin for error.
semiconductors.thesis_status: Structurally bullish, tactically overextended — memory rotation masks logic stall.
semiconductors.key_development: MU hit $1T market cap on UBS PT $1,625 (per Yahoo Finance); SK Hynix also crossed $1T as HBM demand undersupply persists.

big_tech.summary: BigTech is mixed: Microsoft holds near its 50‑day as the OpenAI concentration debate limits upside, while Google and Amazon are extended but lack immediate catalyst triggers. The AI capex spending narrative is intact but increasingly questioned on ROI — hyperscaler earnings in July will be pivotal. PLTR remains the only value‑play in the cluster, trading below its 50‑day and at 64x PE with defense AI tailwinds.
big_tech.thesis_status: Mixed — AI capex confirmed but valuation and credit‑circularity risk temper enthusiasm.
big_tech.key_development: No new hyperscaler capex guidance; MSFT Azure AI demand quality continues to be debated.

interconnected.name: Cadence Design Systems
interconnected.reason: US EDA software export restrictions to China directly affect ASML's China DUV customer base and INTC's foundry competitor dynamics.
interconnected.entry_note: Add to watchlist if ASML's High‑NA EUV orders are cut on China restrictions, triggering a <15% pullback.

interconnected.name: Samsung Electronics
interconnected.reason: Third HBM supplier; its qualification status with NVDA determines whether the HBM duopoly (SK Hynix/MU) tightens or breaks.
interconnected.entry_note: Revisit after MU's June 25 earnings for HBM3E market‑share commentary; a Samsung qualification win could rebalance supply.

interconnected.name: Qualcomm
interconnected.reason: Snapdragon AI edge chips for on‑device inference — a direct read‑through to the AI compute buildout beyond data‑center GPUs.
interconnected.entry_note: Add to watchlist if Cramer comments on edge AI catalysts following TSMC's price hike, and QCOM pulls back 8‑10% from current levels.

interconnected.name: Coherent Corp.
interconnected.reason: NVDA $2B photonics investment alongside LITE — COHR's 6‑inch InP yield challenges contrast with LITE's Greensboro fab buildout.
interconnected.entry_note: Monitor after LITE's next earnings for optical interconnect commentary; COHR becomes interesting on a pullback to 50‑day SMA.

active_risks: US‑Iran Hormuz ceasefire draft MOU reported (per NY Post, May 27); Iran says framework would reopen strait within 1 month, US withdraws blockade, but Trump and allies skeptical.
Fed Chair Warsh faces inflation at 3‑year high with PCE expected 3.9% (per CBS News); Citadel and ECB warn of complacency; US10Y at 4.48%.
Trump administration plans tariffs on USMCA partners (per Reuters, May 26), adding trade friction alongside existing China restrictions.
China targets Japan with rare earth export controls and dual‑use item bans (per Newsweek, May 25), escalating tech supply chain weaponization.
geopolitical.market_impact: The Hormuz ceasefire draft sent WTI tumbling to $90.14, hitting energy but boosting rate‑sensitive tech. However, the Fed's hawkish pivot keeps the 10Y yield elevated, compressing valuations for extended semis like MU and SK Hynix. Singapore banks (D05, O39, U11) remain under NIM pressure from dual US‑SGD rate headwinds.
geopolitical.new_since_yesterday: Iran state TV reported a draft MOU to reopen Hormuz within 1 month (per NY Post, May 27); oil fell sharply. Fed Chair Warsh's inflation‑fighting stance reinforced by Citadel warning (per Bloomberg).
geopolitical.portfolio_action: Hold cash; no new entries until VIX settles below 22 or a decisive Hormuz ceasefire is signed. Selective entry possible on oversold high‑quality names like MSFT or WRD only after technical reset.

portfolio_snapshot.overall_stance: Defensive with extreme divergence in Semis
portfolio_snapshot.risk_posture: 19 of 26 names are hard‑blocked on technical extension; the memory and AI networking melt‑up masks widespread overbought conditions. A Hormuz ceasefire could remove the oil risk premium but the Fed's hawkishness remains the primary headwind for rate‑sensitive sectors.

watch_for_entry.note: ADR risk caps sizing. Bounce above $7.23 on volume with stop at $6.85; sizing R:R 1.8:1. Starter position only.
